notes.title: Alpha-particle Scattering Experiment
points: Performed by Geiger and Marsden under Rutherford's suggestion.
Directed a beam of 5.5 MeV alpha particles at a thin gold foil (thickness 2.1 x 10^-7 m).
Observations: Most particles passed straight through; some were scattered by small angles; a very few (1 in 8000) were deflected by more than 90 degrees.

notes.title: Geiger-Marsden Experiment Setup
notes.caption: Alpha particles from source collimated by lead bricks strike gold foil; scattered particles detected by rotatable detector.

notes.title: Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
points: Most of the space in an atom is empty.
The entire positive charge and most of the mass of the atom are concentrated in a tiny central core called the nucleus.
Electrons revolve around the nucleus in circular orbits like planets around the sun.
Electrons and nucleus are held together by electrostatic force of attraction.

notes.title: Trajectory of Alpha Particles
notes.caption: Paths of alpha particles showing deflection depends on impact parameter b.

notes.label: Impact Parameter
conditions: b is perpendicular distance of velocity vector from nucleus.
K is kinetic energy of alpha particle.

notes.valid_when: At this distance, entire kinetic energy K converts to electrostatic potential energy. Gives an estimate of nuclear size.

notes.title: Drawbacks of Rutherford's Model
points: Accelerated charged particles (orbiting electrons) must radiate energy, leading to spiral path and instability.
Cannot explain the discrete line spectrum observed in atoms.

notes.title: Bohr's Model of Hydrogen Atom
points: Postulate 1 (Stable Orbits): Electron revolves in stable circular orbits without radiating energy.
Postulate 2 (Quantisation Condition): Angular momentum is an integral multiple of h/2π.
Postulate 3 (Frequency Condition): Radiation is emitted when electron jumps from higher energy orbit (Ei) to lower energy orbit (Ef).

notes.label: Bohr's Quantisation Condition
conditions: n is an integer (1, 2, 3...) called principal quantum number.

notes.label: Bohr's Frequency Condition
conditions: Energy difference determines frequency of emitted photon.

notes.latex: r_n = \frac{\epsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \implies r_n \propto n^2
notes.valid_when: For Hydrogen. For hydrogen-like ions (He+, Li++), divide by Z.

notes.title: Bohr Radius
points: The radius of the first orbit (n=1) of hydrogen atom.
Value: a₀ = 0.529 Å.

notes.statement: Total Energy of Electron in nth Orbit
notes.valid_when: Negative sign indicates the electron is bound to the nucleus.

notes.title: Energy Level Diagram
points: Ground State (n=1): Energy = -13.6 eV.
First Excited State (n=2): Energy = -3.4 eV.
Ionisation Energy: Energy required to remove electron from ground state to infinity (+13.6 eV).

notes.title: Energy Levels of Hydrogen
notes.caption: Horizontal lines representing discrete energy states corresponding to n=1, 2, 3...

notes.title: Line Spectra of Hydrogen
points: Emission spectrum consists of discrete lines grouped into series.
Lyman Series: Transitions to n=1 (UV Region).
Balmer Series: Transitions to n=2 (Visible Region).
Paschen, Brackett, Pfund Series: Transitions to n=3, 4, 5 (IR Region).

notes.label: Rydberg Formula
conditions: R is Rydberg constant = 1.097 x 10^7 m^-1.

notes.title: Hydrogen Spectral Series
notes.caption: Arrows showing electron jumps corresponding to Lyman, Balmer, Paschen series.

notes.title: de Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate
points: Electron behaves as a particle wave.
For a stable orbit, the circumference must be an integral multiple of the wavelength (standing wave condition).

notes.label: Quantisation of Orbit
conditions: Using de Broglie relation (lambda = h/mv), this leads to mvr = nh/2pi.

ncert_golden_lines: Rutherford's experiments suggested the size of the nucleus to be about 10^-15 m to 10^-14 m.
An atom has a nearly continuous mass distribution in a Thomson model but has a highly non-uniform mass distribution in a Rutherford model.
Bohr combined classical and early quantum concepts and gave his theory in the form of three postulates.
The negative sign of the total energy of an electron moving in an orbit means that the electron is bound with the nucleus.
De Broglie argued that the electron in its circular orbit, as proposed by Bohr, must be seen as a particle wave.
Only those orbits are stable for which the standing wave is formed.

diagram_descriptors.detailed_description: Alpha particles from source collimated by lead bricks strike gold foil; scattered particles detected by rotatable detector.
Paths of alpha particles showing deflection depends on impact parameter b.
Horizontal lines representing discrete energy states corresponding to n=1, 2, 3...
Arrows showing electron jumps corresponding to Lyman, Balmer, Paschen series.
